skill.name: networkx
skill.description: NetworkX guidance for creating, analyzing, importing, exporting, and visualizing graph data in Python. It helps users choose graph types, algorithms, file formats, generators, and visualization patterns for relationship data.
skill.summary: Create, analyze, import, export, and visualize graph networks with Python NetworkX.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: AI review dismisses the critical heuristic and most static findings as false positives caused by markdown code fences, NetworkX terminology, and documentation examples. The skill is publishable, but it has medium documentation risk because it teaches local file I/O and pickle loading without explicit safety warnings. No evidence found of prompt injection, malicious command execution, credential access, or data exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe Pickle Deserialization Example
medium_findings.description: The I/O reference shows pickle and gpickle loading examples without a warning about untrusted files. This is a documentation-level risk because pickle loading can execute code when users apply the pattern to hostile graph files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly show pickle.dump, pickle.load, write_gpickle, and read_gpickle. The risk depends on users loading untrusted files, so confidence is high for the unsafe pattern but not for malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Local File Read and Write Examples
low_findings.description: The references include examples that read and write local graph, JSON, CSV, database, and image files. These are expected for a NetworkX I/O skill, but users should choose paths carefully to avoid overwriting data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are clearly present and use fixed demonstration filenames. They are legitimate documentation examples, so the concern is operational caution rather than evidence of harmful behavior.

low_findings.title: External Resource Links Are Present
low_findings.description: The skill links to official NetworkX documentation, tutorial, gallery, and GitHub pages. These hardcoded URLs are low risk and do not transmit data from the user environment.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are plain markdown links to recognized NetworkX resources. No code path or instruction sends local data to those URLs.

low_findings.title: Static Command and Crypto Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The numerous external command and weak cryptography alerts come from markdown code fences, graph algorithm names, and NetworkX examples. No evidence found of shell execution, credential access, obfuscated behavior, or malicious network activity in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Representative flagged lines are documentation snippets and graph terminology, not executable skill scripts. A targeted prompt-injection search also found no override or skip-analysis language.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unsafe Pickle Deserialization Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The I/O reference shows pickle and gpickle loading examples without a warning about untrusted files. This is a documentation-level risk because pickle loading can execute code when users apply the pattern to hostile graph files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly show pickle.dump, pickle.load, write_gpickle, and read_gpickle. The risk depends on users loading untrusted files, so confidence is high for the unsafe pattern but not for malicious intent.

content.value_statement: Graph analysis often requires many choices about data structure, algorithms, formats, and visualization. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through practical NetworkX workflows for relationship data.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use Graph, DiGraph, MultiGraph, and MultiDiGraph structures.
Guides shortest path, centrality, clustering, connectivity, matching, and traversal analysis.
Shows how to create classic, random, lattice, tree, social, and bipartite graph models.
Covers graph import and export with edge lists, GraphML, GML, JSON, CSV, pandas, and SQLite.
Provides visualization guidance for matplotlib, layouts, Plotly, PyVis, Graphviz, and publication figures.
Highlights performance choices such as sparse matrices, approximate algorithms, and reproducible random seeds.
limitations: It is reference guidance and does not run NetworkX code by itself.
Large graph performance still depends on available memory and algorithm complexity.
Interactive visualization examples may require optional packages outside NetworkX.
File I/O examples need user-controlled paths and trusted input files.

use_cases.title: Map Entity Relationships
use_cases.description: Create a graph from customer, product, document, or research relationships and inspect its structure.

use_cases.title: Find Important Nodes
use_cases.description: Select centrality, connectivity, community, and path algorithms for influence or dependency analysis.

use_cases.title: Prepare Network Visuals
use_cases.description: Choose layouts, styling, labels, and export formats for clear static or interactive graph views.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Basic Graph
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me model this relationship data as a NetworkX graph. Explain which graph type to use and how to add nodes, edges, and attributes.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Analysis Metrics
prompt_templates.prompt: Given my graph goal and data size, recommend NetworkX metrics for paths, centrality, clustering, and connectivity. Explain the tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Import and Export Graph Data
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a NetworkX import and export workflow for my graph files. Compare edge list, GraphML, JSON, CSV, pandas, and database options.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize a Large Network Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my large NetworkX workflow for algorithm complexity, memory use, reproducibility, and visualization strategy. Suggest safer and faster alternatives.

output_examples.input: I have relationships between employees and projects. What should I build?
output_examples.output: Use a bipartite graph with one node set for employees and one for projects. Add attributes for team, role, and project status.

output_examples.input: I need to identify influential accounts in a follower network.
output_examples.output: Use a directed graph. Compare in-degree centrality, PageRank, and betweenness centrality, then validate results against known domain behavior.

output_examples.input: I want a publication-ready network figure.
output_examples.output: Choose a reproducible layout seed, size nodes by a meaningful metric, simplify labels, export PNG for review, and export PDF for print.

best_practices: Pick the graph class before loading data so direction and parallel edges are represented correctly.
Set random seeds for generated graphs and force-directed layouts to make results reproducible.
Use trusted files for imports, especially pickle and gpickle formats.
anti_patterns: Do not use an undirected graph when edge direction changes the analysis result.
Do not run expensive exact centrality algorithms on very large graphs without estimating cost.
Do not load graph files from unknown sources with pickle-based formats.

faq.question: Does this skill execute NetworkX code?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to help users write or review NetworkX workflows.

faq.question: Which graph types does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers Graph, DiGraph, MultiGraph, and MultiDiGraph, including common use cases for each structure.

faq.question: Can it help with graph algorithms?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers shortest paths, centrality, clustering, connectivity, communities, flow, matching, coloring, and traversal.

faq.question: Can it help with file formats?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers edge lists, adjacency lists, GraphML, GML, JSON, CSV, pandas, NumPy, SciPy, SQLite, and visualization formats.

faq.question: Can it help visualize networks?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for matplotlib layouts, styling, labels, Plotly, PyVis, Graphviz, and publication exports.

faq.question: What security caution matters most?
faq.answer: Avoid loading pickle or gpickle graph files unless the file source is trusted, because pickle can execute unsafe content.
